name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ أُوحِىَ إِلَىَّ أَنَّهُ ٱسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا سَمِعْنَا قُرْءَانًا عَجَبًا (١)
```

```english
Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that a team of the jinn listened [to the Quran] and they said, “Indeed we heard a wonderful Quran, (1)
```

```arabic
يَهْدِىٓ إِلَى ٱلرُّشْدِ فَـَٔامَنَّا بِهِۦ ۖ وَلَن نُّشْرِكَ بِرَبِّنَآ أَحَدًا (٢)
```

```english
which guides to rectitude. Hence, we have believed in it and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord. (2)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّهُۥ تَعَـٰلَىٰ جَدُّ رَبِّنَا مَا ٱتَّخَذَ صَـٰحِبَةً وَلَا وَلَدًا (٣)
```

```english
Exalted be the majesty of our Lord; He has taken neither any spouse nor son. (3)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّهُۥ كَانَ يَقُولُ سَفِيهُنَا عَلَى ٱللَّهِ شَطَطًا (٤)
```

```english
The foolish ones among us used to speak atrocious lies concerning Allah. (4)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّآ أَن لَّن تَقُولَ ٱلْإِنسُ وَٱلْجِنُّ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (٥)
```

```english
We thought that humans and jinn would never utter any falsehood concerning Allah. (5)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّهُۥ كَانَ رِجَالٌ مِّنَ ٱلْإِنسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجَالٍ مِّنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَزَادُوهُمْ رَهَقًا (٦)
```

```english
Indeed some persons from the humans would seek the protection of some persons from the jinn, thus only adding to their rebellion. (6)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّهُمْ ظَنُّوا۟ كَمَا ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَبْعَثَ ٱللَّهُ أَحَدًا (٧)
```

```english
They thought, just as you think, that Allah will not raise anyone from the dead. (7)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا لَمَسْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ فَوَجَدْنَـٰهَا مُلِئَتْ حَرَسًا شَدِيدًا وَشُهُبًا (٨)
```

```english
Indeed we made for the heaven and found it full of mighty sentries and flames. (8)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا كُنَّا نَقْعُدُ مِنْهَا مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلسَّمْعِ ۖ فَمَن يَسْتَمِعِ ٱلْـَٔانَ يَجِدْ لَهُۥ شِهَابًا رَّصَدًا (٩)
```

```english
We used to sit in its positions to eavesdrop, but anyone listening now finds a flame waiting for him. (9)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا لَا نَدْرِىٓ أَشَرٌّ أُرِيدَ بِمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ أَرَادَ بِهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ رَشَدًا (١٠)
```

```english
We do not know whether ill is intended for those who are on the earth, or whether their Lord intends good for them. (10)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا مِنَّا ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ وَمِنَّا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ كُنَّا طَرَآئِقَ قِدَدًا (١١)
```

```english
Among us some are righteous and some are otherwise: we are various sects. (11)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّآ أَن لَّن نُّعْجِزَ ٱللَّهَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَن نُّعْجِزَهُۥ هَرَبًا (١٢)
```

```english
We know that we cannot frustrate Allah on the earth, nor can we frustrate Him by fleeing. (12)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا لَمَّا سَمِعْنَا ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ ۖ فَمَن يُؤْمِنۢ بِرَبِّهِۦ فَلَا يَخَافُ بَخْسًا وَلَا رَهَقًا (١٣)
```

```english
When we heard the [message of] guidance, we believed in it. Whoever that has faith in his Lord will fear neither privation nor oppression. (13)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّا مِنَّا ٱلْمُسْلِمُونَ وَمِنَّا ٱلْقَـٰسِطُونَ ۖ فَمَنْ أَسْلَمَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ تَحَرَّوْا۟ رَشَدًا (١٤)
```

```english
Among us some are Muslims and some of us are perverse.” ’ Those who submit [to Allah]—it is they who pursue rectitude. (14)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا ٱلْقَـٰسِطُونَ فَكَانُوا۟ لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَبًا (١٥)
```

```english
As for the perverse, they will be firewood for hell. (15)
```

```arabic
وَأَلَّوِ ٱسْتَقَـٰمُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلطَّرِيقَةِ لَأَسْقَيْنَـٰهُم مَّآءً غَدَقًا (١٦)
```

```english
If they are steadfast on the path [of Allah], We shall provide them with abundant water, (16)
```

```arabic
لِّنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن يُعْرِضْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِۦ يَسْلُكْهُ عَذَابًا صَعَدًا (١٧)
```

```english
so that We may test them therein, and whoever turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, He will let him into an escalating punishment. (17)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah refers to the story of a group of jinn who listened to the Quran, believed in it, and affirmed the fundamental principles of its knowledge. From this, it proceeds to record the prophethood of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and points toward the oneness of Allah (تعالى) in His Lordship and toward the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of all beings to Allah for judgment*. The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by its context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *قُلْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ نَفَرٌ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَقالُوا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً يَهْدِي إِلَى الرُّشْدِ* » **Say, ‘It has been revealed to me that a team of jinn listened [to the Quran] and they said, “Indeed we have heard a wonderful Quran, (1) which guides to rectitude”** — This is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to relate the story to his people. The One who reveals is Allah, the Glorified. The object of "listened" (اسْتَمَعَ), which is the Quran, is omitted because the context indicates it. A "team" (النفر) refers to a group of three to nine according to the popular view, though some say up to forty.

"Wonderful" (عجباً) refers to that which invites amazement because it deviates from the established norm for such things. They described the Quran as wonderful because it is a speech that transcends the norm in its wording and meaning, brought by an unlettered man who could neither read nor write.

"Rectitude" (الرشد) is the attainment of reality (إصابة الواقع), and it is the opposite of error (الغي). The Quran’s guidance to rectitude is its call toward beliefs and actions that ensure the actual happiness of those who adhere to them.

The meaning is: O Messenger, say to the people: "It has been revealed (أُوحِيَ)" — meaning Allah revealed — "to me that a group of jinn listened to the Quran and said" — to their people when they returned to them — "Indeed, we have heard a recited speech that transcends the norm, guiding toward knowledge consisting of beliefs and actions; in adhering to them lies the attainment of reality and the triumph of true happiness."

## (A Discourse on the Jinn)

The jinn are a type of creation hidden from our senses. The Noble Quran affirms their existence and mentions that their species was created before the human species, and that they were created from fire just as man was created from dust. The Exalted said: « *وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نارِ السَّمُومِ* » **And the jinn We created before from a smokeless fire.** (Al-Hijr: 27).

They live, die, and are resurrected like humans. The Exalted said: « *أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ فِي أُمَمٍ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ* » **They are the ones against whom the word has become due, along with the nations that passed away before them of jinn and humans.** (Al-Ahqaf: 18).

Among them are males and females who multiply through procreation and lineage. The Exalted said: « *وَأَنَّهُ كانَ رِجالٌ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجالٍ مِنَ الْجِنِّ* » **Indeed some persons from the humans used to seek the protection of some persons from the jinn.** (Al-Jinn: 6).

They possess consciousness (شعور) and will (إرادة), and they are capable of swift movements and arduous tasks, as seen in the stories of Solomon (عليه‌السلام), the subjugation of the jinn to him, and the story of the Queen of Sheba.

They are legally accountable (مكلفون) like humans; among them are believers and disbelievers, and among them are the righteous and the wicked. The Exalted said: « *وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ* » **I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me.** (Adh-Dhariyat: 56). And the Exalted said: « *إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً يَهْدِي إِلَى الرُّشْدِ فَآمَنَّا بِهِ* » **“Indeed we have heard a wonderful Quran, which guides to rectitude, so we have believed in it.”** (Al-Jinn: 1-2). And He said: « *وَأَنَّا مِنَّا الْمُسْلِمُونَ وَمِنَّا الْقاسِطُونَ* » **“Among us are those who submit [to Allah], and among us are the deviant.”** (Al-Jinn: 14). And He said: « *وَأَنَّا مِنَّا الصَّالِحُونَ وَمِنَّا دُونَ ذلِكَ* » **“Among us are the righteous, and among us are those otherwise.”** (Al-Jinn: 11). And the Exalted said: « *قالُوا يا قَوْمَنا إِنَّا سَمِعْنا كِتاباً أُنْزِلَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مُوسى مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ يا قَوْمَنا أَجِيبُوا داعِيَ اللهِ* » **They said, ‘O our people! Indeed we have heard a Book concluded after Moses, confirming what was before it, guiding to the truth and to a straight path. O our people! Respond to Allah’s inviter.’** (Al-Ahqaf: 30-31), among other details of their states indicated by the Quranic verses.

It appears from the speech of the Exalted that Iblis is from the jinn and that he has offspring and a tribe. The Exalted said: « *كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ* » **He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command.** (Al-Kahf: 50). And the Exalted said: « *أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِي* » **Will you then take him and his offspring for guardians besides Me?** (Al-Kahf: 50). And the Exalted said: « *إِنَّهُ يَراكُمْ هُوَ وَقَبِيلُهُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا تَرَوْنَهُمْ* » **Indeed he and his tribe see you from where you do not see them.** (Al-A'raf: 27).

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *فَآمَنَّا بِهِ وَلَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِرَبِّنا أَحَداً* » **“so we have believed in it, and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord.”** — This is an account of their faith in the Quran and their attestation that it is the truth. Their saying: "and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord" is an emphasis of the meaning of their faith; that their faith in the Quran is faith in Allah who revealed it, for He is their Lord, and that their faith in Him (تعالى) is a faith of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — wherein they will never associate anyone with Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّهُ تَعالى جَدُّ رَبِّنا مَا اتَّخَذَ صاحِبَةً وَلا وَلَداً* » **“and that—exalted be the majesty of our Lord—He has taken neither any spouse nor son.”** — "Majesty" (الجد) has been interpreted as greatness (العظمة) and also as fortune (الحظ). The verse serves to emphasize their saying: "and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord."

The popular recitation is "that he" (أَنَّهُ) with the *fathah* on the *alif*. It has also been recited with the *kasrah* (إِنَّهُ) in this verse and the twelve subsequent verses until His saying: "And that if they..." (وَأَنْ لَوِ اسْتَقامُوا). The *fathah* is more likely because the context of the verses clearly shows they are part of the speech of the jinn.

As for the recitation with *fathah*, its grammatical justification is not without obscurity. Some have justified it by saying the clause "and that He..." (وَأَنَّهُ) etc., is coordinated with the genitive pronoun in His saying "so we believed in it" (فَآمَنَّا بِهِ). The implied meaning would be: "and we believed that exalted be the majesty of our Lord..." etc. Thus, it is an account of their faith in the negation of a spouse and a son for Him (تعالى), contrary to what the idolaters claim.

This is only sound according to the Kufan grammarians who allow coordination with a connected genitive pronoun. As for the Basran grammarians who do not allow it, some, such as al-Farra, al-Zajjaj, and al-Zamakhshari, have justified it by saying it is coordinated with the place (محل) of the preposition and its object, which is the accusative (النصب). For His saying: "so we believed in it" (فَآمَنَّا بِهِ) carries the meaning of "we attested to it" (صدقناه). The implied meaning would be: "and we attested that exalted be the majesty of our Lord..." etc. The forced nature (التكلف) of this explanation is not hidden.

Others justified it by assuming a suppressed preposition in the coordinated clause, which is a regular occurrence with the particles *an* (أَنْ) and *anna* (أَنَّ). The implied meaning would be: "we believed in it and in [the fact] that exalted be the majesty of our Lord..." etc.

A rebuttal to all these views—whether coordination with the genitive pronoun, its place, or the suppression of the preposition—is that the meaning only remains sound in His saying: "and that exalted be the majesty of our Lord..." etc., and His saying: "and that the foolish ones among us used to speak..." etc. However, for the rest of the verses beginning with *anna*, such as His saying: "and that we thought that humans and jinn would never speak..." etc., and His saying: "and that there were men from among the humans..." etc., and His saying: "and that we touched the heaven...", it is certainly not correct. There is no sense in saying: "We believed or attested that we thought that humans and jinn would never speak against Allah a lie," or "We believed or attested that there were men from among the humans who sought protection..." etc., or "We believed or attested that we touched the heaven..." etc.

This problem is only resolved by resorting to what some have mentioned: if the *fathah* in the first two verses is justified by assuming "faith" or "attestation," then each of the remaining verses should be justified by an appropriate assumed meaning.

Some justified the *fathah* by saying that His saying: "and that He (تعالى)..." etc., and the other verses beginning with *anna* are coordinated with His saying: "that a team... listened" (أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ) etc.

The flaw in this is not hidden, for its result would be that the verses are in the position of reporting what was revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding their sayings. He reported their saying: "Indeed we heard a wonderful Quran, so we believed in it" as a report of their speech, and then related the rest of their sayings verbatim. The meaning would then be: "It was revealed to me that a team of jinn listened and said we heard such and such, and it was revealed to me that exalted be the majesty of our Lord..." etc., and "It was revealed to me that the foolish ones among us used to speak..." until the end of the verses.

The rebuttal to this is that if the words "that he" (أَنَّهُ), "that they" (أَنَّهُمْ), and "that we" (أَنَّا) at the beginning of the verses were not part of their related speech, they would be redundant and disruptive to the discourse. If they were part of their speech related verbatim, then the related content of the combination of *anna* and what follows it would not be a complete sentence and would require an assumed completion for the relation to be sound. Coordinating it with His saying: "that he listened" (أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ) would be of no use in this regard. So do not be heedless.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّهُ كانَ يَقُولُ سَفِيهُنا عَلَى اللهِ شَطَطاً* » **“and that the foolish ones among us used to speak monstrous lies against Allah.”** — "Foolishness" (السفه), as mentioned by al-Raghib, is a lightness of the soul due to a deficiency in intellect. "Monstrous lies" (الشطط) is speech that is far removed from the truth.

The verse is also in the sense of emphasizing their saying: "we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord." By "their foolish ones," they mean those who preceded them among the polytheist jinn. It has been said that it refers to Iblis, who is from the jinn, but this is far from the context of his saying: "the foolish ones among us used to speak..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّا أَنْ لَنْ تَقُولَ الْإِنْسُ وَالْجِنُّ عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً* » **“and that we thought that humans and jinn would never speak any lie against Allah.”** — This is an admission from them that they thought humans and jinn were truthful in what they said and would not lie against Allah. When they found them to be polytheists and heard them ascribing a spouse and a son to Him (تعالى), they submitted to it and followed them in what they said, thus becoming polytheists like them, until they heard the Quran and the truth was revealed to them. In this is their refutation of the polytheists among humans and jinn.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّهُ كانَ رِجالٌ مِنَ الْإِنْسِ يَعُوذُونَ بِرِجالٍ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَزادُوهُمْ رَهَقاً* » **“Indeed some persons from the humans used to seek the protection of some persons from the jinn, and that only added to their rebellion.”** — Al-Raghib said: "Seeking protection" (العوذ) is to take refuge in another. He also said: "The matter overwhelmed him" (رهقه الأمر) means it covered him by force. "Rebellion" (الرهق) has been interpreted as sin, tyranny, fear, evil, and humiliation or weakness; these are interpretations based on the necessary implications of the meaning.

What is meant by humans seeking protection from the jinn—as has been said—is that an Arab man, when stopping in a valley during his night journey, would say: "I seek protection with the master of this valley from the evil of the foolish ones of his people." It is narrated from Muqatil that the first to seek protection from the jinn were a people from Yemen, then the Banu Hanifa, and then it spread among the Arabs.

It is not unlikely that seeking protection from the jinn refers to seeking their help in objectives through soothsaying (الكهانة). To this returns what was narrated from some that the meaning was: men from among the humans used to seek protection with men from among the jinn because of the jinn and their harm and annoyance.

The two pronouns in His saying: "so they increased them" (فَزادُوهُمْ) — the first refers to the men from the humans and the second to the men from the jinn. The meaning is: the men of the humans increased the men of the jinn in rebellion (رهقاً) by their taking refuge in them, so the men of the jinn became arrogant, tyrannical, and sinful. The reverse is also possible: the first pronoun refers to the men of the jinn and the second to the men of the humans. The meaning would then be: the men of the jinn increased the men of the humans in rebellion, i.e., sin and tyranny, or humiliation and fear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّهُمْ ظَنُّوا كَما ظَنَنْتُمْ أَنْ لَنْ يَبْعَثَ اللهُ أَحَداً* » **“and that they thought, just as you thought, that Allah will not raise anyone [from the dead].”** — The pronoun in "that they" (أَنَّهُمْ) refers to the men from the humans, and the address in "you thought" (ظَنَنْتُمْ) is to their people from the jinn. What is meant by "raising" (البعث) is the raising of a Messenger with a message, for the polytheists deny that. It has also been said that it refers to the revival after death. The context of the following verses supports the first view.

It is narrated from some that this verse and the one before it are not from the speech of the jinn, but rather the speech of the Exalted as an interpolation between the verses containing the speech of the jinn. Accordingly, the pronoun in "that they" (أَنَّهُمْ) would refer to the jinn and the address "you thought" (ظَنَنْتُمْ) to the people. However, this is far from the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا لَمَسْنَا السَّماءَ فَوَجَدْناها مُلِئَتْ حَرَساً شَدِيداً وَشُهُباً* » **“Indeed we touched the heaven and found it full of a powerful guard and meteors.”** — "Touching the heaven" (لمس السماء) is to approach it by ascending toward it. "Guard" (الحرس)—as has been said—is a collective noun for "guardians," which is why it is described with a singular adjective. What is meant by "powerful guard" are strong protectors who repel whoever wants to eavesdrop from it; hence it is joined with "meteors" (الشهب), which are their weapons.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا كُنَّا نَقْعُدُ مِنْها مَقاعِدَ لِلسَّمْعِ فَمَنْ يَسْتَمِعِ الْآنَ يَجِدْ لَهُ شِهاباً رَصَداً* » **“We used to sit in its positions to eavesdrop, but anyone who listens now finds a meteor lying in wait for him.”** — Joining the beginning of this verse to the previous one indicates that the filling of the heaven with a powerful guard and meteors is something that occurred recently, and that they previously used to sit in positions in the heaven to listen to the speech of the angels. The end of the verse, as a consequence of all that preceded, indicates that whoever among us listens now by sitting in a position for eavesdropping will find a meteor whose quality is that it is lying in wait (رَصَداً) for him, to be pelted with it by the guard.

Thus, the sum of the two verses yields the report that they came upon a new celestial event coinciding with the revelation of the Quran and the mission of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), which is the prevention of the jinn from receiving the news of the heaven by eavesdropping.

Among the strange arguments is what is narrated from some that in these two verses is a refutation of those who claimed that the pelting (الرجم) occurred after the mission of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), because the appearance of His saying: "it was filled with a guard" (مُلِئَتْ حَرَساً) suggests that the new event is the filling and the abundance of the guard, not the existence of the guard itself. And the appearance of His saying: "We used to sit in its positions to eavesdrop" suggests that "we used to find some positions therein empty of guards and meteors, but now all positions are filled, so whoever listens now finds a meteor lying in wait."

This is refuted by the fact that if the intent of the two verses were to report the filling of the heaven with guards and the increase of their number such that no empty positions are found—whereas they existed before—then the negation in His saying: "anyone who listens now finds a meteor lying in wait for him" should have been directed at eavesdropping from all positions as opposed to affirming eavesdropping from some of those positions, not the negation of eavesdropping altogether.

Even if we grant that the intent is the absolute negation of eavesdropping—which is sufficient for that—the purpose of the speech being the report of the "filling" with guards while some positions were empty before, as well as the qualification of His saying: "anyone who listens" with "now" (الْآنَ), indicates the occurrence of a new matter in the pelting of the jinn, which is the comprehensiveness of the pelting for them in whatever position they sit and the absolute prevention from eavesdropping after they used to eavesdrop from some positions without prevention. This amount is sufficient for the claimant in what he claims.

Let it be noted that the implication of the verse is the occurrence of the pelting of the jinn with a meteor lying in wait, which is different from the occurrence of the celestial meteor itself; this is clear. Thus, there is no relevance to what has been said that celestial meteors were among the existing atmospheric phenomena before the time of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the revelation of the Quran.

The reason for its lack of relevance is that what appears from the Quran is the occurrence of the pelting of the devils from among the jinn with meteors, without addressing the occurrence of the origin of the meteors. Some of what pertains to this matter has already been discussed in the commentary on the beginning of Surah as-Saffat.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا لا نَدْرِي أَشَرٌّ أُرِيدَ بِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ أَرادَ بِهِمْ رَبُّهُمْ رَشَداً* » **“We do not know whether ill is intended for those who are on the earth, or whether their Lord intends to guide them to rectitude.”** — "Rectitude" (الرشد) with two *fathahs* or with a *dammah* and *sukun* is the opposite of error. The indefiniteness of "rectitude" (رَشَداً) is to indicate a type, i.e., a type of rectitude.

This is an expression from them of ignorance and bewilderment regarding what they witnessed of the matter of pelting and the prevention of the devils of the jinn from accessing the news of the heaven. However, they realized that this was for a reason pertaining to the people of the earth, either evil or good. If it were good, it would be a type of guidance and happiness for them; hence they substituted "good"—which is the opposite of evil—with "rectitude." This is supported by their saying: "their Lord intends for them," which suggests mercy and care.

They explicitly mentioned the Agent for the intention of rectitude and omitted Him in the case of evil out of etiquette (أدباً). Evil is not intended from His side (تعالى) except for those who deserve it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا مِنَّا الصَّالِحُونَ وَمِنَّا دُونَ ذلِكَ كُنَّا طَرائِقَ قِدَداً* » **“Among us are the righteous, and among us are those otherwise; we have been of different factions.”** — Righteousness (الصلاح) is the opposite of wickedness (الطلاح). What is meant by "otherwise" (دون ذلِكَ) is that which is close to it in rank—as has been said. It is apparent that "otherwise" (دون) means "other than." This is supported by His saying: "we have been of different factions (طَرائِقَ قِدَداً)," which indicates division and dispersal. "Factions" (الطرائق) is the plural of "way" (طريقة), which is the trodden and traveled path. "Different" (القدد) refers to pieces, the plural of "piece" (قدة), from the root meaning "to cut" (القد). The ways were described as "different" because each one of them is cut off from the others, leading its traveler to an end different from where others end. To this meaning returns the interpretation of "different" as dispersed and scattered ways.

It appears that what is meant by His saying: "the righteous" (الصَّالِحُونَ) is those who are righteous according to their primary nature in social interaction and dealings, rather than those who are righteous according to faith. Had the righteousness of faith been intended, it would have been more appropriate to mention it after the upcoming discussion of their faith when they heard the guidance.

Some mentioned that His saying: "different factions" (طَرائِقَ قِدَداً) is in the accusative case as an adverb of place, i.e., "in different factions," which are the dispersed and scattered schools of thought. Others said it assumes a suppressed possessed noun, i.e., "possessors of factions." It is not unlikely that it is a metaphor (استعارة) by likening themselves in their difference and disparity to paths cut off from one another, leading to scattered ends.

The meaning is: among us are those who are naturally righteous and among us are those other than that; we were in different schools of thought, or possessors of different schools, or like paths cut off from one another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا ظَنَنَّا أَنْ لَنْ نُعْجِزَ اللهَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَنْ نُعْجِزَهُ هَرَباً* » **“We know that we cannot frustrate Allah on the earth, nor can we frustrate Him by flight.”** — "Thought" (الظن) here is certain knowledge (العلم اليقيني). It is more appropriate that the meaning of His saying: "we cannot frustrate Allah on the earth" is frustrating Him (تعالى) by overcoming Him in what He wills therein, and that is by causing corruption on the earth and disrupting the order that runs within it; for their corruption, if they were to corrupt, is part of the Decree (القدر). The meaning of His saying: "nor can we frustrate Him by flight" is frustrating Him (تعالى) by fleeing from Him if He seeks them, such that they escape Him and He is unable to seize them.

It has been said: the meaning is we cannot frustrate Him (تعالى) while being on the earth, nor can we frustrate Him by fleeing to the heaven; i.e., we cannot frustrate Him neither on the earth nor in the heaven. This is as you see [weak].

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا لَمَّا سَمِعْنَا الْهُدى آمَنَّا بِهِ فَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ بِرَبِّهِ فَلا يَخافُ بَخْساً وَلا رَهَقاً* » **“When we heard the guidance, we believed in it. So whoever believes in his Lord shall fear neither any diminution nor any rebellion.”** — What is meant by "the guidance" (الهدى) is the Quran, considering what it contains of guidance. "Diminution" (البخس) is a reduction by way of injustice. "Rebellion" (الرهق) is the overwhelming of something disliked.

The *fa* in His saying: "So whoever believes" (فَمَنْ يُؤْمِنْ) is for consequence (التفريع), and it is the derivation of the cause from the effect to provide the proof for their faith in the Quran without hesitation or delay.

The summary of the meaning is: when we heard the Quran, which is the guidance, we hastened to believe in it without pause, because whoever believes in it has believed in his Lord, and whoever believes in his Lord fears neither a decrease in good nor the overwhelming of something disliked, such that he would refrain from hastening and urgency and deliberate before embarking upon it lest he fall into diminution or rebellion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنَّا مِنَّا الْمُسْلِمُونَ وَمِنَّا الْقاسِطُونَ فَمَنْ أَسْلَمَ فَأُولئِكَ تَحَرَّوْا رَشَداً* » **“Among us are those who submit [to Allah], and among us are the deviant. Those who submit—they are the ones who have sought rectitude.”** — What is meant by "submission" (الإسلام) is the surrender of the matter to Allah (تعالى). Thus, the "Muslims" are those who surrender the matter to Him, obeying Him in what He wills and commands. The "deviant" (القاسطون) are those who incline toward falsehood. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "The *qasit* is the one who deviates from the truth, and the *muqsit* is the one who is just toward the truth."

The meaning is: we, the assembly of jinn, are divided into those who submit to the command of Allah, obeying Him, and those who deviate from submission to the command of Allah, which is the truth.

His saying: "Those who submit—they are the ones who have sought rectitude" — "seeking" (تحري) something is to pursue and intend it. The meaning is: those who submitted, they intended to attain reality and triumph through the truth.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَمَّا الْقاسِطُونَ فَكانُوا لِجَهَنَّمَ حَطَباً* » **“As for the deviant, they shall be fuel for hell.”** — They will be punished by their own kindling and burning, like the deviant among humans. The Exalted said: « *فَاتَّقُوا النَّارَ الَّتِي وَقُودُهَا النَّاسُ* » **“then beware of the Fire whose fuel is humans.”** (Al-Baqarah: 24).

Many have considered His saying: "Those who submit—they are the ones..." until His saying: "...fuel for hell" to be a continuation of the speech of the jinn addressing their people. It has also been said that it is from the speech of Him (تعالى) addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَأَنْ لَوِ اسْتَقامُوا عَلَى الطَّرِيقَةِ لَأَسْقَيْناهُمْ ماءً غَدَقاً لِنَفْتِنَهُمْ فِيهِ* » **“If they are steadfast on the path [of Allah], We shall provide them with abundant water, (16) so that We may test them therein.”** — "That" (أَنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy *anna*. What is meant by "the path" (الطريقة) is the path of Islam. "Steadfastness" (الاستقامة) upon it is to adhere to it and remain firm upon what it necessitates of faith in Allah and His signs.

"Abundant water" (ماءً غَدَقاً) is a large amount of it. It is not unlikely to be understood from the context that His saying: "We shall provide them with abundant water" is a metaphor intended to mean an expansion in sustenance (الرزق). This is supported by His saying after it: "so that We may test them therein."

The meaning is: and that if they—meaning the jinn and humans—were steadfast on the path of submission to Allah, We would provide them with abundant sustenance to test them in their sustenance. Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: « *وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ* » **“If the people of the towns had believed and been God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven and the earth.”** (Al-A'raf: 96).

The verse is from the speech of Him (تعالى), coordinated with His saying at the beginning of the Surah: "that he listened" (أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ) etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** « *وَمَنْ يُعْرِضْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِ يَسْلُكْهُ عَذاباً صَعَداً* » **“But whoever turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, He will let him into a mounting punishment.”** — "Mounting punishment" (عذاباً صَعَداً) is that which ascends over the one being punished and overcomes him. It has also been said it is a grueling punishment.

Turning away from the remembrance of Allah is a necessary consequence of the lack of steadfastness on the path, and it is the root cause for entering the punishment. Therefore, it was placed in its stead to indicate the original cause for entering the Fire.

This is also the reason for the shift (الالتفات) from the first-person plural to the third-person in His saying: "remembrance of his Lord" (ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِ). The apparent requirement was to say: "Our remembrance." This is because the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) is the original principle for punishing those who turn away from His remembrance (تعالى); thus it was placed in the position of the first-person plural pronoun to indicate the original principle, just as "turning away from remembrance" was placed in the position of "lack of steadfastness" to indicate the cause.

It has been said: His saying: "He will let him into" (يَسْلُكْهُ) implies the meaning of "He will make him enter," which is why it took a second object. The meaning is clear.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, al-Wahidi narrated from Sa'id ibn Jubayr from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not recite to the jinn nor did he see them. The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) set out with a group of his companions—heading toward the market of 'Ukaz, and a barrier had been placed between the devils and the news of the heaven. The devils returned to their people and they said: 'What is the matter with you?' They replied: 'A barrier has been placed between us and the news of the heaven—and meteors have been sent against us.' They said: 'This is only because of something that has happened—so strike the easts of the earth and its wests.'

The group that took the direction of Tihamah passed by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)—while he was heading toward the market of 'Ukaz—and he was praying the dawn prayer with his companions. When they heard the Quran, they listened to it and said: 'This is what has come between us and the news of the heaven.' They returned to their people and said: 'Indeed we have heard a wonderful Quran, which guides to rectitude, so we have believed in it, and we will never ascribe any partner to our Lord.' Then Allah revealed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): 'Say, it has been revealed to me that a team of jinn listened...'"

It was also narrated by al-Bukhari and Muslim in the *Sahih*.

**I say:** Al-Qummi narrated something similar in his *Tafsir*, and we have cited the narration in the commentary on Surah al-Ahqaf under His saying: « *وَإِذْ صَرَفْنا إِلَيْكَ نَفَراً مِنَ الْجِنِ* » **“When We stayed a team of jinn toward you...”** etc.

However, the appearance of his narration is that the group regarding whom the verses of Surah al-Ahqaf were revealed is the same group regarding whom this Surah was revealed. The appearance of the verses of the two Surahs does not harmonize with that. For the appearance of their saying reported in Surah al-Ahqaf: "Indeed we have heard a Book concluded after Moses (confirming what was before it), guiding to the truth..." indicates that they were believers in Moses and attestors to the Torah. The appearance of the verses of this Surah is that they were polytheists who did not recognize prophethood. This necessitates that the two groups are distinct, unless the apparent meaning is contested.

In the same source, from 'Alqamah ibn Qays, who said: "I said to Abdullah ibn Mas'ud: 'Who among you was with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) on the night of the jinn?' He said: 'None of us was with him. We missed him one night while we were in Mecca—so we said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) has been assassinated or carried away." We set out looking for him in the mountain passes and met him coming from the direction of Hira. We said: "O Messenger of Allah, where were you? We were anxious for you," and we said to him: "We spent the worst night a people could spend when we missed you." He said to us: "An inviter from the jinn came to me, so I went to recite the Quran to them." He took us and showed us their tracks and the tracks of their fires. As for any of us accompanying him, no.'"

In the same source, from al-Rabi' ibn Anas, who said: "Allah (تعالى) has no 'majesty' (جد) [in the sense of a grandfather or ancestor]; rather, the jinn said it out of ignorance—so Allah, the Glorified, related it as they said it." This has been narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (عليه السلام).

**I say:** The "majesty" (الجد) negated from Him (تعالى) is fortune and luck.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Ali (عليه السلام) in a tradition: "The jinn approached him while the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was in Batn al-Nakhl—and they apologized that they had thought, just as you thought, that Allah would not raise anyone. Seventy-one thousand of them approached him—and pledged allegiance to him for fasting, prayer, zakat, pilgrimage, jihad, and sincerity toward Muslims—and they apologized that they had spoken monstrous lies against Allah."

**I say:** Their pledge of allegiance to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for fasting, prayer, etc., is attested by their saying reported at the beginning of the Surah: "so we believed in it" and their saying: "When we heard the guidance, we believed in it." As for the manner of their performance of these, especially zakat and jihad, it is unknown to us. Their first apology mentioned is not without obscurity.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with his chain to Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far about the saying of Allah: 'Indeed some persons from the humans used to seek the protection of some persons from the jinn, and that only added to their rebellion.' He said: 'A man would go to a soothsayer—to whom the devil would reveal—and say: "Tell the devil: So-and-so has sought protection with you."'"

In the same source: regarding His saying (تعالى): "So whoever believes in his Lord shall fear neither any diminution nor any rebellion," he said: "Diminution (البخس) is reduction, and rebellion (الرهق) is punishment."

The Scholar (عليه السلام) was asked about the believers among the jinn: "Will they enter Paradise?" He said: "No, but Allah has enclosures (حظائر) between Paradise and the Fire—wherein will be the believers of the jinn and the sinful among the Shi'a."

**I say:** Perhaps what is meant by these "enclosures" are some degrees of Paradise that are below the Paradise of the righteous.

Know that it has been mentioned in some narrations from the paths of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) the application of what is in the verses regarding "guidance" and "the path" to the guardianship (ولاية) of Ali (عليه السلام); this is of the nature of *jari* (الجري) — *the application of a verse to a specific instance or person* — and is not part of the literal commentary (التفسير) at all.
